sections.label: Situation Overview
sections.title: US-Iran Ceasefire Agreed Just Hours Before Trump Deadline
paragraphs: The United States and Iran reached a two-week ceasefire agreement on Tuesday, less than two hours before President Trump's 8pm deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz or face massive infrastructure strikes. The deal, mediated by Pakistan, requires Iran to immediately allow safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for suspended US-Israeli bombing campaigns.

Under the agreement, Trump cancelled threats to destroy Iranian power plants and bridges that would have constituted potential war crimes under international law. Markets responded immediately, with oil prices plunging 13% and stock futures surging on news of the temporary halt to hostilities that have gripped global energy markets since February.
key_facts: Ceasefire announced with 1 hour 45 minutes to spare before Trump's deadline
Iran must reopen Strait of Hormuz for safe passage during 2-week truce
Oil prices fell 13% immediately following ceasefire news
Israel also agreed to suspend bombing operations against Iran
sections.australia_relevance: Australia had prepared for potential fuel rationing and economic recession if the conflict escalated. The Albanese government expressed relief at the pause in hostilities but warned Australians that fuel price impacts will persist regardless of the ceasefire outcome.

sections.label: Background
sections.title: 40-Day War Stemming from Failed Nuclear Negotiations
paragraphs: The US-Israel military campaign against Iran began on February 28 after nuclear negotiations in Oman collapsed. Trump had demanded complete dismantlement of Iran's nuclear program and uranium enrichment capabilities, while Iran insisted on retaining domestic enrichment rights under international law. The talks breakdown led to coordinated strikes that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and installed his son Mojtaba as the new leader.

Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz - through which 20 percent of global oil transits - has been the conflict's most economically damaging aspect. Over 2,000 people have been killed in Iran according to Iranian authorities, while regional casualties include over 1,500 in Lebanon and 110 in Iraq since the war began.
key_facts: War began Feb 28 after nuclear talks failed in Oman
Ali Khamenei killed in strikes, replaced by son Mojtaba
Strait of Hormuz shipping down 90% from normal levels
Over 2,000 killed in Iran since conflict started

sections.label: Diplomacy
sections.title: Pakistan Mediates Deal as UN Resolution Blocked
paragraphs: Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif emerged as the key mediator who brokered the ceasefire after intensive shuttle diplomacy between Washington and Tehran. Sharif has invited both US and Iranian delegations to Islamabad for comprehensive peace talks on Friday, April 10, with aims to negotiate a permanent settlement to end the 40-day conflict.

At the United Nations, Russia and China vetoed a Security Council resolution that would have provided international naval protection for Strait of Hormuz shipping. The 11-4 vote with two abstentions failed to reach the required threshold, with Moscow and Beijing arguing the resolution was biased against Iran while ignoring alleged US-Israeli aggression.
key_facts: Pakistan PM Sharif credited as key ceasefire broker
Peace talks scheduled in Islamabad for April 10
UN Security Council resolution vetoed by Russia-China
Iran proposed 10-point peace plan rejected by Trump as 'not good enough'
sections.australia_relevance: Australia supported the failed UN resolution and continues to maintain that Iran must cease attacks on merchant shipping. Australia has not been invited to participate in the Pakistan-mediated peace talks despite being affected by the shipping disruptions.

sections.label: Economy & Energy
sections.title: Global Fuel Crisis Persists Despite Ceasefire Hope
paragraphs: Despite the ceasefire announcement, energy analysts warn the global fuel crisis will persist for months due to damaged Middle Eastern infrastructure and disrupted supply chains. Only 53 ships transited the Strait of Hormuz last week - the highest since the war began but still 90 percent below normal traffic levels of approximately 130 daily transits.

The conflict has triggered what experts call the largest oil supply disruption in history, with Brent crude trading above $100 per barrel before falling on ceasefire news. European countries face potential fuel shortages by month-end, while Asian refineries that normally process Persian Gulf crude have been forced to source alternative supplies from as far as Mexico and the US Gulf Coast.
key_facts: Strait shipping remains 90% below normal despite recent uptick
Brent crude fell from $117 to $103 on ceasefire news
Europe facing potential fuel shortages within weeks
At least 70 large oil tankers stranded off Singapore waiting for safe passage
sections.australia_relevance: Australian fuel prices averaged $2.40 per litre this week after government excise cuts, down from peaks of $2.53 but still 35% above pre-war levels. The government maintains 39 days of petrol reserves and 29 days of diesel, with Energy Minister Chris Bowen confirming fuel deliveries are assured until mid-April despite some cancelled tanker shipments.

sections.label: What to Watch
sections.title: Peace Talks and Hormuz Reopening Key to Sustained Calm
paragraphs: The immediate focus shifts to whether Iran will fully reopen the Strait of Hormuz during the two-week ceasefire period, as required under the agreement. Any violations could trigger resumed US-Israeli strikes, while successful implementation may lead to the comprehensive peace talks scheduled in Islamabad on Friday.

Key indicators include daily ship transit numbers through Hormuz, oil price movements, and whether both sides can agree on nuclear restrictions and sanctions relief during the Pakistani-mediated negotiations. Trump is scheduled to meet with NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte on Wednesday, potentially signalling broader Western diplomatic coordination on the Iran issue.
key_facts: Iran must immediately allow safe Hormuz passage under ceasefire terms
Comprehensive peace talks scheduled Islamabad April 10
Trump meeting NATO chief Wednesday may signal alliance coordination
Any ceasefire violations could restart bombing campaigns within hours
sections.australia_relevance: Australia will monitor fuel supply chains and shipping routes closely during the ceasefire period. Prime Minister Albanese has indicated Australia supports the diplomatic process while maintaining travel warnings against Iran, Israel and Lebanon remain at the highest 'Do Not Travel' level.
